company.name: Exelon
company.description: Exelon is a regulated U.S. transmission and distribution utility holding company delivering electricity and natural gas through local operating subsidiaries.

overview.title: Regulated utility footprint
paragraphs: Exelon operates through ComEd, PECO, BGE, Pepco, Delmarva Power, and Atlantic City Electric, serving major metropolitan and regional utility territories around Chicago, Philadelphia, Baltimore, Washington, D.C., Delaware, Maryland, and southern New Jersey.

The company is primarily a wires-and-pipes utility after the separation of Constellation, so its economics are tied to regulated rate base growth, reliability investments, customer demand, and state and federal utility oversight rather than merchant generation.

overview.title: Registry relevance
paragraphs: Exelon is useful for the registry because its strongest moat is not a consumer brand or software network effect but a regulated monopoly franchise over critical physical infrastructure.

That makes disruption less about replacing the utility overnight and more about reducing dependence on centralized grid control through distributed energy resources, open control layers, microgrids, demand response, and neighborhood-scale thermal infrastructure.

moatNarrative: Exelon's moat is high because electric and gas distribution utilities are territorial, capital-intensive, regulated infrastructure businesses with franchise rights, embedded rate-base recovery, and essential-service obligations. Customers cannot usually choose a duplicate local wire or gas delivery network in the same way they can switch software vendors.

The moat is not absolute. Public utility commissions can disallow costs, distributed energy can reduce load growth or reshape grid needs, and resilience or affordability pressure can force utilities to open more of the coordination layer to aggregators, customers, and community-scale assets.
decentralizationNarrative: Electric service has a meaningful decentralization path because solar, batteries, controllable loads, EV chargers, open energy management systems, and microgrids can shift some reliability and balancing work to households, campuses, neighborhoods, and aggregators.

Natural gas delivery is harder to decentralize as a like-for-like service because the pipeline network is inherently physical, safety-regulated, and commodity-dependent. The more credible decentralizing route is substitution: electrification, geothermal or thermal networks, efficiency, and open energy data that lets customers coordinate away from fossil gas demand over time.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 201-225.

moat.rationale: Regulated local transmission, distribution, and gas utility franchises create high barriers through physical infrastructure, rate regulation, reliability obligations, and territorial service models.

decentralizability.rationale: Electric distribution can be partially decentralized through DERs, demand response, microgrids, and open controls, but monopoly distribution networks and safety-critical operations keep the core service hard to replace.

profitability.rationale: Exelon's 2025 results show positive GAAP earnings and utility earnings growth, but regulated utilities normally trade exceptional margins for stability, capital spending, and commission oversight.

peRatio.rationale: StockAnalysis reported a current Exelon P/E ratio of 16.91 in late May 2026; market multiples move continuously, so this is a point-in-time registry input.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap and StockAnalysis both placed Exelon's market capitalization in the high-$40B range in late May 2026; the registry uses approximately $47.3B for this refresh.

products.name: Electric utility service
products.summary: Exelon's electric utilities deliver power across regulated local networks in Illinois, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Washington, D.C., Delaware, and New Jersey.
products.whyItMatters: Electric distribution is the operational choke point between centralized generation, distributed generation, buildings, EV charging, demand response, and resilience planning.
replacementSketch: A realistic replacement path starts at the edge: open energy management, customer-owned storage, local generation, and demand response reduce how much intelligence must live inside a single utility control stack.
Over time, interoperable microgrids and open DER coordination could make the incumbent utility more of a regulated reliability backstop and less of the sole allocator of local energy flexibility.

alternatives.name: OpenEMS
alternatives.summary: OpenEMS is an open-source energy management platform for coordinating batteries, renewable generation, EV charging, heat pumps, tariffs, and edge/backend energy controls.

openness.rationale: The project publishes source code and documentation and describes itself as a free and open-source energy management system.

decentralizationFit.rationale: OpenEMS is designed around edge systems and decentralized energy assets, making it a strong fit for customer- and site-level energy coordination.

readiness.rationale: The project reports production, pilot, lab, and community usage, but utility-grade deployment still depends on hardware integration, cybersecurity, and regulatory acceptance.

costLeverage.rationale: Open control software can reduce vendor lock-in and integration cost for DER orchestration, though physical grid and hardware costs remain substantial.

alternatives.name: GridLAB-D
alternatives.summary: GridLAB-D is an open-source smart-grid simulation platform used to model distribution networks, distributed resources, loads, tariffs, and resilience scenarios.

openness.rationale: GridLAB-D documentation and repositories identify it as open-source software for smart-grid simulation.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It does not deliver power directly, but it helps communities, researchers, and operators model DER and microgrid alternatives outside proprietary utility planning tools.

readiness.rationale: GridLAB-D is established in research and planning contexts, though it is a modeling tool rather than an operational utility replacement.

costLeverage.rationale: Open modeling can lower planning and analysis costs, but it does not remove the capital cost of wires, protection equipment, or field operations.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open DER flexibility market
disruptionConcepts.summary: Homes, buildings, EV chargers, batteries, and community solar systems expose verified flexible capacity through open energy protocols and local aggregators, allowing distribution constraints and peak demand to be met by many small assets instead of only utility-owned infrastructure upgrades.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The utility remains responsible for reliability, but a larger share of balancing and peak management shifts to customer-owned distributed energy resources coordinated through interoperable markets.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through open participation and multi-party DER coordination rather than through Bitcoin. Customers and aggregators can contribute flexibility without a single proprietary control vendor owning the market interface.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Customers enroll devices through open EMS or aggregator software; utilities or distribution operators publish constraints or dispatch needs; aggregators bid verified load reduction, storage discharge, or generation response into local programs.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Baseline methods, interval meter data, device telemetry, event logs, and settlement audits constrain fake curtailment. Open protocols such as OpenADR reduce vendor lock-in, but final settlement still depends on trusted metering and regulator-approved market rules.
failureModes: Baselines can be gamed if customers or aggregators inflate expected consumption before events.
Distribution operators may limit access if cybersecurity, interconnection, or liability rules are not mature.
Open software alone does not solve physical grid congestion, protection coordination, or field maintenance.
adoptionPath: Start with demand response and managed charging programs in constrained feeders.
Integrate open EMS platforms for batteries, solar, EV chargers, and heat pumps at homes and commercial sites.
Expand from event-based demand response to feeder-level flexibility procurement and community microgrid services.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept directly shifts dispatchable flexibility from centralized utility assets toward many customer-owned DERs and aggregators.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: OpenADR and open EMS tools support the coordination layer, but utility tariffs and settlement rules still determine whether the market works.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The underlying devices and protocols exist, but feeder-level operation requires cybersecurity, telemetry quality, interconnection reform, and regulatory approval.

incumbentPressure.rationale: DER flexibility can defer some capital investment and reduce utility control over customer energy behavior, but it is unlikely to eliminate the regulated distribution franchise.

disruptionConcepts.name: Community microgrid commons
disruptionConcepts.summary: Neighborhoods, campuses, and critical facilities use open planning tools, open EMS software, shared storage, and local generation to operate as islandable microgrids during outages while remaining interconnected during normal conditions.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Reliability investment becomes more contestable when communities can plan and operate some resilience capacity locally instead of relying only on utility-scale restoration and centralized capital plans.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The central mechanism is cooperative and technical decentralization, not Bitcoin. The key shift is from one utility-operated reliability stack to shared local assets governed by municipalities, campuses, cooperatives, or neighborhood energy groups.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Participants pool capital for generation, batteries, switches, and controls; open simulation tools model outages and operating modes; a local operator or cooperative schedules resources and coordinates interconnection with the utility.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Engineering studies, interconnection approvals, metered production, battery state-of-charge telemetry, and periodic islanding tests verify that the microgrid can deliver promised resilience. Cheating is constrained by physical meters and operational tests, but governance capture remains possible.
failureModes: Upfront capital and permitting may be too difficult for many communities.
Poorly governed microgrids could duplicate infrastructure costs or shift costs to nonparticipants.
Islanded operation requires protection, controls, and maintenance capabilities that open software cannot replace.
adoptionPath: Use open planning and simulation tools for schools, shelters, hospitals, and municipal facilities.
Build shared solar-plus-storage or CHP-free resilience hubs with open EMS controls.
Connect multiple sites into neighborhood-scale microgrids that provide both outage resilience and grid services.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Community microgrids decentralize both physical resilience assets and some operational decision-making.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The model has a clear cooperative coordination path, but local governance, finance, and utility interconnection rules are hard constraints.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Open tools and DER hardware exist, but safe islanding, protection coordination, and project finance make deployment materially harder than software adoption.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Microgrids can reduce dependence on utility restoration and defer some upgrades, but most still interconnect with and rely on the incumbent distribution system.

products.name: Natural gas utility service
products.summary: Exelon distributes natural gas through PECO, BGE, and Delmarva Power in parts of Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Delaware.
products.whyItMatters: Gas distribution is a regulated infrastructure business with safety-critical pipelines, but building electrification and thermal networks can reduce long-term dependence on fossil gas delivery.
replacementSketch: The near-term open alternative is not a parallel gas network; it is better customer-side energy visibility, electrification planning, and coordinated demand reduction.

A stronger long-term path replaces portions of gas demand with heat pumps, building efficiency, and neighborhood thermal networks that can be governed as public, cooperative, or utility-regulated shared infrastructure.

alternatives.name: Home Assistant Energy Management
alternatives.summary: Home Assistant's energy dashboard can track electricity, gas, and water usage through open integrations, giving households local control over energy visibility and automation.

openness.rationale: Home Assistant is an open-source home automation platform with public documentation and source code.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It decentralizes monitoring and automation at the household level, but it does not replace the physical gas distribution network.

readiness.rationale: Home Assistant is mature for consumer automation and energy dashboards, though gas metering depends on local meter hardware and integrations.

costLeverage.rationale: Open household monitoring can support efficiency and electrification decisions, but savings depend on appliances, tariffs, and building upgrades.

alternatives.name: OpenEnergyMonitor
alternatives.summary: OpenEnergyMonitor provides open-source tools and hardware-oriented software for monitoring energy use and understanding household or building energy systems.

openness.rationale: The project publishes open-source energy monitoring tools and repositories.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It gives users local measurement and insight, but it is an instrumentation layer rather than a full gas delivery replacement.

readiness.rationale: OpenEnergyMonitor is practical for monitoring deployments, though user skill and hardware setup determine readiness.

costLeverage.rationale: Measurement can reveal waste and support electrification planning, but major cost leverage requires equipment changes beyond the monitoring stack.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open building electrification coordination
disruptionConcepts.summary: Customers use open energy monitors, home automation, tariff-aware controls, and contractor marketplaces to sequence heat-pump adoption, weatherization, and appliance replacement, reducing gas throughput without waiting for a centralized gas utility transition plan.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Gas utilities lose some long-run demand and rate-base certainty if customers coordinate electrification decisions with open data, local installers, and interoperable controls.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through customer-owned measurement and local decision-making. Bitcoin is not central because the core bottleneck is physical equipment replacement and building retrofit coordination, not censorship-resistant settlement.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Households collect usage data locally, compare retrofit options, coordinate contractor bids, and automate electric loads around tariffs and grid conditions through open home energy systems.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Smart meter data, gas meter pulse readings, equipment invoices, building permits, and before-after weather-normalized consumption constrain false savings claims. The main weakness is that comfort, weather, and occupancy can make savings attribution noisy.
failureModes: Retrofit costs can be too high for renters and low-income households without financing or policy support.
Poor installation quality can reduce heat-pump performance and damage trust.
Gas fixed costs may be shifted onto remaining customers if departures are not managed equitably.
adoptionPath: Start with open monitoring of gas and electric consumption.
Prioritize weatherization, heat-pump water heaters, induction cooking, and HVAC replacements at natural equipment turnover points.
Coordinate neighborhood or municipal procurement to lower installation cost and improve contractor accountability.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept decentralizes energy measurement and retrofit decisions to households and communities, though the physical gas network remains centralized while it is used.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Household data and local procurement are credible, but contractor quality, financing, and split incentives are persistent coordination problems.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Monitoring and electrification technologies exist today, but adoption is constrained by building stock, panel capacity, appliance replacement cycles, and local labor.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Electrification can pressure gas throughput over time, but it is gradual and partly mediated by regulators that manage utility cost recovery.

disruptionConcepts.name: Neighborhood thermal network transition
disruptionConcepts.summary: Blocks or districts replace portions of gas heating demand with shared thermal energy networks, geothermal loops, and heat pumps coordinated as community or utility-regulated infrastructure.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Natural gas distribution is pressured not by a competing gas pipe but by shared thermal infrastructure that can reuse utility planning capabilities while moving heat supply away from fossil fuel combustion.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralizing role is neighborhood-scale ownership and operation of thermal assets. Bitcoin is not a natural fit because trust hinges on engineering performance, cost allocation, and regulated access rather than digital settlement.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Building owners, municipalities, utilities, and regulators identify candidate districts, pool capital for loop infrastructure, allocate costs through tariffs or cooperative dues, and dispatch heat pumps and shared thermal resources based on measured demand.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Thermal meters, utility billing data, engineering commissioning tests, and public regulatory filings verify service delivery and cost allocation. Collusion risk is constrained by transparent tariffs and public oversight, but monopoly behavior can reappear if governance is weak.
failureModes: Thermal networks need dense loads and coordinated construction, so many service territories will not pencil out quickly.
Incumbent gas cost recovery can conflict with rapid gas-line retirement.
Poor governance could recreate a local monopoly without meaningful customer control.
adoptionPath: Pilot thermal networks around campuses, public housing, downtown districts, or gas-main replacement zones.
Use open performance data and standardized thermal metering to compare costs with gas-main renewal.
Scale successful districts into regulated or cooperative thermal utility franchises that retire selected gas infrastructure.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Thermal networks move heat supply to district-scale infrastructure and can support cooperative or municipal governance, though they are still physical networks.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The model has policy and pilot precedents, but multi-building construction, tariffs, and ownership allocation are hard coordination problems.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Feasible in dense or planned districts, but expensive and slow for broad retrofit across existing gas utility territories.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Thermal networks could reduce gas distribution demand in targeted zones, but adoption is too slow and site-specific to threaten the whole franchise quickly.
